Surah Al-Masad: Commentary on Ayahs 1-5

Ayah 1

تَبَّتْ يَدَآ أَبِى لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ

May the hands of Abu Lahab be ruined, and ruined is he.

This surah addresses two specific individuals: Abu Lahab and his wife. Understanding their profiles helps us grasp the timeless lessons embedded in these verses.

Abu Lahab, whose actual name was Abd al-Uzza, was given this nickname meaning “Father of the Flame.” The Arabic practice of using “Abu” (father of) in nicknames doesn’t always indicate literal fatherhood—it associates a person with a particular quality or characteristic. Just as Abu Hurayrah (RA) means “the cat man” rather than “father of a cat,” Abu Lahab means “the flame man” or “possessor of the flame.”

The nickname refers to the flickering, dancing part of a fire—the brilliant red-orange flames themselves (lahab), not the fire as a whole. This nickname carries multiple implications: some accounts describe him as having a reddish complexion or reddish hair, while others note his explosive, inflammatory temper. There’s scholarly debate about whether this name existed before the Quran or was given by the Quran itself.

Abu Lahab was one of the wealthiest and most respected figures in Makkah. He served as a treasurer and custodian of the Kaaba, responsible for collecting and managing donations—funds that some suggest he occasionally misappropriated for personal gain. Beyond his religious role, he was a successful businessman known for his striking appearance and high social status.

His connection to the Prophet ﷺ makes this condemnation particularly significant. Abu Lahab was the Prophet’s direct uncle from the Banu Hashim family. He was also the Prophet’s father-in-law, as two of the Prophet’s daughters were married to two of Abu Lahab’s sons before Islam. Historical accounts even mention that when the Prophet ﷺ was born, Abu Lahab celebrated joyously, freeing a servant girl who brought him the news and slaughtering animals in celebration. The cruelty and enmity came later, after Islam.

His transformation into an enemy was complete. As the Prophet’s next-door neighbor, he would throw trash onto the Prophet’s property whenever he heard Quranic recitation. Eventually, he demanded that his sons divorce the Prophet’s daughters, threatening to disown them if they refused. They complied, and one son even went further, spitting at the Prophet ﷺ and publicly declaring his disbelief in Surah An-Najm. When the Prophet’s son Qasim passed away, Abu Lahab emerged celebrating, declaring that Muhammad’s name would be “cut off” with no sons to continue his legacy.

The phrase “tabbat yada” literally means “may both hands deteriorate and fall apart slowly.” The root word “tabba” describes something once rigid and strong that gradually becomes broken, soft and useless—like a road that becomes smooth through constant trampling. This isn’t an instant destruction but a gradual degeneration.

Why are hands specifically mentioned? In the Quran, hands represent earning and accomplishment—what a person builds with their efforts. Allah ﷻ is saying that everything Abu Lahab worked to build—his empire, reputation, wealth and status—would slowly but surely fall apart. The mention of both hands carries additional significance: in Arab warrior tradition, the right hand held the sword (offense) and the left held the shield (defense). By saying both hands fall apart, Allah ﷻ indicates that Abu Lahab would have no power to attack the Prophet ﷺ anymore, nor any defense when consequences came.

The verse continues: “wa tabba” (and ruined is he). First his hands deteriorate, then he himself. His accomplishments crumble, then his very being. Historical accounts describe that after the Battle of Badr, he developed a severe plague. His corpse lay rotting for three days because people feared catching the disease. Eventually, they pushed his body into a ditch with long sticks, refusing to touch it, and covered it with stones. The man who once commanded respect through intimidation died in the ugliest, most degrading manner—a slow, painful deterioration, exactly as the verse foretold.

This opening establishes a crucial principle: no matter how powerful someone appears, no matter their wealth or status or connections, when they dedicate themselves to opposing truth and harming those who carry it, their downfall is inevitable. The verse also demonstrates Allah’s special love for His Messenger ﷺ—those who hurt the Prophet ﷺ face divine consequences unlike others.

Ayah 2

مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَمَا كَسَبَ

His wealth will not avail him or that which he gained.

This ayah can be understood as either a rhetorical question—“What did his wealth benefit him?”—or as a definitive statement: “His wealth did not benefit him at all, nor what he earned.”

The distinction between “maluhu” (his wealth) and “ma kasaba” (what he earned) offers important insight. His wealth represents the tangible, material assets he possessed—property, camels, gold, physical resources. But what he earned encompasses something broader: reputation, status, respect, fear in the eyes of others, social standing. These intangible gains cannot be purchased at a store or quantified in monetary terms, yet they represent real power.

There’s also a progression here. His wealth enabled him to earn these intangible things. His money bought him influence, which earned him respect, which earned him authority. But when consequences arrived, none of it mattered. The material wealth couldn’t save him, and everything that came as a result of that wealth—all those earned advantages—also disappeared.

Some scholars note another dimension: Abu Lahab had both legitimate earnings and illegitimate wealth obtained through embezzlement and misappropriation of funds donated for the Kaaba. Neither the lawfully earned nor the unlawfully acquired came to his aid. Everything he accumulated, regardless of its source, proved utterly worthless.

This ayah teaches a timeless lesson about shallow relationships built on materialism. When people respect you solely because of money or material possessions, that respect evaporates when the money goes. Abu Lahab surrounded himself with people drawn to his wealth and status. When his power began deteriorating, so did his social circle. These weren’t genuine relationships built on character and values—they were transactional arrangements masquerading as connections.

The ayah also addresses a specific incident. When confronted with the Prophet’s message, Abu Lahab reportedly declared: “If what my nephew says is true, I’ll ransom myself with my wealth and my children.” Notice what he said—he would give up his money and even sacrifice his children rather than accept the truth. This reveals how obsession with wealth and status can corrupt even natural instincts. When people become so fixated on material accumulation and social standing, they view even their own children primarily as assets. When those children don’t meet expectations or become liabilities, parents can discard them—a complete inversion of natural parental love.

The mention of “what he earned” also refers to his sons, as children were considered part of one’s earnings in pre-Islamic Arabian culture. When two of his sons later embraced Islam, the Companions noted that these “earnings” of Abu Lahab brought him no benefit—they couldn’t save him from his fate. The one son who remained a disbeliever obviously provided no benefit either.

This ayah strips away the illusion that material success equals security or salvation. It exposes the fundamental emptiness of a life built solely on wealth accumulation and status seeking. No matter how much someone gathers in this world, when they face divine consequences for their choices, none of it provides protection or assistance.

Ayah 3

سَيَصْلَىٰ نَارًا ذَاتَ لَهَبٍ

He will enter to burn in a Fire of blazing flame.

The verb “sayasla” indicates he will be cast into or exposed to fire. The word choice connects directly to his nickname—he possessed lahab (flame) in this world, and now he will be thrown into a fire that possesses lahab in the Hereafter. The flames that defined his worldly personality—whether his appearance, his inflammatory temper, or his party lifestyle—now become the instrument of his punishment.

The description “dhati lahab” (possessing flame/blaze) emphasizes a fire characterized by intense, engulfing flames. This isn’t just any fire—it’s a fire whose defining quality is that very characteristic Abu Lahab was named for. There’s a profound irony here: the quality that perhaps made him attractive or intimidating in worldly terms becomes the nature of his torment.

This connects to a hadith where the Prophet ﷺ described his relationship with the ummah: “My example and the example of my ummah is like a man who lit a fire, and small creatures began falling into the flame.” Flames are attractive from a distance. They draw things toward them. Similarly, shallow people living superficial lives can appear very attractive. Their lifestyle looks appealing, seductive even. You want to be around them, follow them on social media, emulate them. But when you get close, the ugliness reveals itself and everything burns.

How many celebrities, athletes and politicians appeared admirable from afar, only for scandals to emerge when people got closer? The flame looked beautiful from a distance, but proximity revealed the destructive reality. Abu Lahab embodied this principle—an attractive, wealthy, powerful figure whose true nature brought ruin to himself and those who followed him.

The punishment fitting the crime reflects a Quranic principle. He ignited flames of hostility against the Prophet ﷺ and Islam. He inflamed situations, provoked conflicts and spread the fire of opposition. Now he enters a fire that matches what he created in the world. The flames he generated through his actions become the literal flames that consume him.

Ayah 4

وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ حَمَّالَةَ ٱلْحَطَبِ

And his wife, the carrier of firewood.

Abu Lahab’s wife Umm Jamil (whose name was Arwa) came from the prominent family of Umayyah. She took great pride in her lineage and noble status, constantly flaunting her high-class background. Wherever she went, she made sure people knew she belonged to elite society. She adorned herself with heavy jewelry and expensive accessories, embodying the image of wealth and status.

Her nickname “Umm Jamil” means “mother of beauty” or “possessor of beauty”—she cultivated an aura of being the epitome of beauty itself. She represents an archetype: the trophy wife of a powerful man, the one who does the showing off on his behalf. While Abu Lahab wouldn’t parade around displaying his wealth, she served that function. She talked about what jewelry he bought her, what luxuries they possessed. She was the visible display of their affluence.

But her support for her husband went deeper than appearances. She was one of his most adamant supporters in opposing the Prophet ﷺ. She actively worked to undermine Islam, composing and commissioning poetry insulting the Prophet ﷺ. When she brought her prized pearl necklace and declared she would auction it to fund the campaign against Muhammad ﷺ, she revealed her true priorities. She backed Abu Lahab not out of ideological conviction, but because his power and wealth sustained her lifestyle. If Islam succeeded and his power diminished, where would her jewelry and status come from? She supported him for purely materialistic reasons.

The description “hammalat al-hatab” (carrier of firewood) operates on multiple levels. The word “hammala” (carrier) uses a pattern that indicates a professional—someone who performs an action repeatedly with expertise. A “khabbaz” (baker) bakes bread continuously; a “qassab” (butcher) cuts meat continuously. Similarly, “hammala” suggests she professionally carried—again and again and again.

In the literal sense, the poorest women in Arabian society would go into the wilderness, gather dry sticks and branches, bundle them up, carry them on their backs and sell them. They knew which wood burned best. They did this repeatedly, constantly—it was their survival. By calling Umm Jamil a “hammala,” Allah ﷻ equates this woman of the highest social class with the lowest class of society. She who saw herself as above everyone else is described using terminology for the most menial labor.

But the real significance is metaphorical. Just as those women carried physical firewood to burn, she carried the firewood of slander, gossip, propaganda and evil speech. She knew exactly what words to bring that would ignite conflicts and fuel her husband’s flames. She was expert at carrying harmful speech—gossip, backbiting, insults—and using it to inflame situations. In the worldly sense, she lit fires of hostility through her words. In the Hereafter, she literally carries firewood to fuel the flames consuming both her and her husband.

The image carries profound poetic justice. In this world, she backed him in his opposition to Islam, providing the fuel for his inflammatory behavior. In the next world, she continues this role—endlessly carrying fuel for the fire they both burn in. The punishment mirrors the crime with perfect precision.

The description also serves as a deliberate insult to her ego. For a woman who prided herself on class and status, who looked down on others, who flaunted her nobility—being called a firewood carrier represented the ultimate degradation. She spent her life trying to elevate herself above others; Allah ﷻ identifies her with the lowest station imaginable.

Ayah 5

فِى جِيدِهَا حَبْلٌ مِّن مَّسَدٍۭ

Around her neck is a rope of twisted fiber.

The word “jid” refers to the neck, specifically used to describe a woman’s neck when adorned with jewelry. This was exactly what Umm Jamil loved to display—her decorated neck with expensive necklaces. Arabic poetry often used “jid” when describing beautiful women showing off their jewelry. She was known for flaunting her pearl necklaces and precious ornaments. This wasn’t just any neck—it was a neck meant to be seen, meant to impress, meant to display wealth.

Allah ﷻ now describes what’s on that decorated neck: a rope. Not a pearl necklace, not gold or jewels, but a rope. He replaced her prized adornments with something crude and humiliating. But this isn’t just any rope—it’s “hablun min masad,” a rope of twisted fiber.

“Masad” refers to perfectly twisted rope where the strands are wound so tightly there are no loose ends. The tighter the twist, the stronger the rope. Significantly, masad also describes fire-resistant material. Ancient builders used masad-quality rope for construction because if fire broke out, these ropes wouldn’t easily burn, preventing structural collapse. So the rope around her neck won’t burn away—it will remain, continuing to fulfill its purpose.

What is that purpose? A twisted rope around the neck chokes. When you twist a rope around someone’s neck, it constricts their airway and they cannot speak. Her crime was her speech—the slander, the poetry against the Prophet ﷺ, the propaganda, the inflammatory words. Now that same channel for speech is closed off. She who composed verses against the Prophet ﷺ, who could instantly produce eloquent insults, will be completely silenced. The rope chokes her voice entirely.

Some scholars interpret “habl” here to refer to the jugular vein itself—the internal “rope” in the neck through which voice emerges. In this reading, it’s not just an external rope but something internal that’s twisted and constricted, making speech impossible from within.

The phrase “fi jidiha” (in her neck, or inside her neck) supports this interpretation. It’s not just around the decorated exterior, but penetrating inside that neck, twisted within it. She used that neck to project her voice, to display her status through jewelry, to command attention. Now it becomes the site of her torment and silence.

The fitting nature of this punishment exemplifies Quranic justice. She adorned her neck with expensive necklaces, particularly that pearl necklace she offered to auction for the anti-Islam campaign. Allah ﷻ speaks directly to that necklace: You want to fund opposition with your jewelry? Here’s your replacement—a rope of twisted fiber. You used your articulate speech to harm? You’ll be choked into permanent silence.

This final image completes the portrait of the couple’s downfall. Abu Lahab, who inflamed conflicts with his presence and actions, will burn in flames. His wife, who carried the fuel for his flames through her speech, will carry literal firewood while choking on the very rope that silences her voice. Every element of who they were in opposition to the Prophet ﷺ becomes precisely how they are punished.

Universal Lessons from Surah Al-Masad

This surah presents several archetypal profiles that transcend its historical specificity. It’s not merely about two individuals from seventh-century Makkah—it reveals patterns of human behavior that manifest across all times and places.

One crucial question emerges: Why would the Quran dedicate an entire surah to these two people? The Roman and Persian empires existed at this time, containing far more powerful individuals. Countless people throughout the world possessed greater wealth, influence and status than Abu Lahab. Why single him out?

The answer lies in understanding that the size of a disease isn’t measured by external circumstances but by its internal reality. This surah captures the enormity of spiritual disease within someone’s heart regardless of their material position. Abu Lahab and his wife lived in their own small world—a desert town with mud homes, no grand monuments or towers to speak of. Yet within that limited world, their arrogance, materialism and hatred reached colossal proportions deserving divine attention.

We all live in our own worlds. Your world consists of the people you know, the job you have, the money you possess, the house you live in, the experiences you undergo. Within your world, you could manifest the same qualities as Fir’awn without owning pyramids or ruling Egypt. You could embody Abu Lahab’s arrogance within a one-bedroom apartment. The location and scale don’t matter—what matters is the condition of your heart.

This surah picks up on a theme from earlier in the Quran’s sequence. Surah Al-Humazah described the fire of Hell climbing up into hearts, as if hearts themselves are criminals requiring the fire to penetrate them. That surah portrayed someone who hoards wealth obsessively, counts it constantly, uses wealth to humiliate others and talks behind people’s backs. Abu Lahab and his wife embody those characteristics.

The couple represents several archetypal patterns. Abu Lahab exemplifies multiple enemy types: the noble enemy with potential (like Abu Jahl, whom the Prophet ﷺ saw could have been amazing had he accepted guidance), the verbal opponent who uses arguments and insults, and ultimately the worst kind—one who hates completely, holds no honor even toward family, shows no restraint in cruelty. When the Prophet’s infant son Qasim died, Abu Lahab celebrated publicly, dancing and rejoicing where the grieving family could see and hear. Even enemies typically show some human decency, but he transcended all boundaries.

His wife represents another archetypal pattern. The Quran presents four scenarios for women in various environments:

First, a righteous woman in a righteous environment—like Maryam (AS), who was herself pure and raised in purity.

Second, a righteous woman in a terrible environment—like the wife of Fir’awn, who prayed for rescue from her oppressive surroundings despite being trapped in Fir’awn’s household.

Third, a corrupt woman in a righteous environment—like the wives of Nuh (AS) and Lut (AS), who betrayed their prophet-husbands despite being in the households of the righteous.

Fourth, a corrupt woman in a corrupt environment—Umm Jamil. Two negatives that didn’t make a positive. She backed him, he supported her, and together they spiraled deeper into evil.

These patterns teach us that environment doesn’t solely determine character. Good people can thrive in bad environments (the wife of Fir’awn). Bad people can emerge from good environments (the wives of Nuh and Lut). We cannot assume that providing children a good environment guarantees good outcomes—plenty have fallen despite that. Conversely, being trapped in difficult circumstances doesn’t doom someone to corruption.

The final universal lesson concerns marriage itself. Allah ﷻ highlighted this particular evil of a couple working together to cause harm. Don’t allow your marriage to become one where the wife carries firewood for the husband’s flames—where she backs up his worst impulses, fuels his arrogance, supports his cruelty, helps him harm others. The purpose of marriage should be developing goodness together, creating sadaqah jariyah (continuous good deeds) through your partnership and the righteous generations that follow. When marriage instead becomes a vehicle for joint wrongdoing, it transforms into a curse rather than a blessing.

Watch out for the qualities Allah ﷻ highlighted in these figures: arrogance, pride, materialism, shallowness, slander, backbiting, putting others down. If your constant preoccupation revolves around what someone said, what you want your spouse to say to someone, which posts they should make, which messages they should send—if you’re continuously fueling conflicts through gossip and harmful speech—you’re manifesting the hammala al-hatab characteristic. That’s a serious problem requiring immediate attention.

On the other side, if you’re someone with an explosive temper, someone nobody can approach because you’re always “on fire,” someone who uses anger and intimidation to maintain control and prevent criticism—you’re displaying Abu Lahab tendencies. These people aren’t mentioned so we merely curse them. They’re mentioned so we learn not to become like them.

The surah also affirms the sacred status of the Prophet ﷺ. Those who hurt Allah’s Messenger ﷺ draw special divine attention and face particular consequences. Defending the Prophet’s honor and showing love and respect for him represents a core Islamic principle, demonstrated by Allah ﷻ Himself dedicating this surah to addressing those who opposed him.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from the characteristics of Abu Lahab and his wife, and may He grant us righteous spouses who help us toward goodness rather than fueling flames of harm. May He make our marriages sources of continuous blessings for ourselves, our families and future generations.
